_meta.authoringNotes: Umbrella SEO page covering the cornea section of aihealz. Numbers cross-checked against AAO Cornea PPP 2023, Eye Bank Association of America 2023 Statistical Report, Whitcher 2001 Bull WHO microbial keratitis review, Wollensak 2003 Am J Ophthalmol corneal cross-linking trial, and Krachmer 2010 Cornea textbook. Drug names checked against UpToDate Lexicomp.

h1Title: Corneal Disorders: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Corneal disorders cover every disease that compromises the clear front window of the eye, from a minor abrasion that heals overnight to a microbial ulcer that threatens sight within 48 hours. The cornea is responsible for about two-thirds of the eye's focusing power, so even subtle changes in its shape, transparency, or surface produce blurred vision, glare, halos, and pain. The umbrella spans infectious keratitis, inherited dystrophies such as Fuchs and keratoconus, mechanical injuries, ulcers, scars, neovascularization, and stem cell deficiency. Corneal disease is the fourth leading cause of global blindness after cataract, glaucoma, and age-related macular degeneration, and most of that burden is preventable or treatable when patients reach a cornea specialist early.

keyStats.prevalence: Corneal opacity affects roughly 4.2 million people worldwide as a cause of vision loss (WHO 2019); Fuchs endothelial dystrophy is present in about 4% of US adults over 40
keyStats.demographics: Microbial keratitis disproportionately affects contact-lens wearers (relative risk ~30x), agricultural workers, and tropical populations; keratoconus typically begins in the second decade of life
keyStats.avgAge: Keratoconus onset 10-25 years; Fuchs dystrophy presents after age 50; microbial keratitis spans all ages
keyStats.globalCases: 1-3 million new microbial keratitis cases per year globally (Whitcher 2001 WHO Bulletin); roughly 80,000 corneal transplants performed annually in the United States (EBAA 2023)

definition: Corneal disorders (ICD-10: H18.9 unspecified disorder of cornea, with subcategories H16 keratitis, H17 corneal scars and opacities, H18.5 hereditary corneal dystrophies, and H18.6 keratoconus) are diseases of the transparent avascular dome that forms the front surface of the eye. The adult cornea is about 540 micrometers thick centrally and is built from five layers: epithelium, Bowman's membrane, stroma (90% of the thickness), Descemet's membrane, and a single endothelial monolayer that pumps water out to maintain clarity. Disease at any layer produces a recognizable clinical pattern: epithelial defects cause pain and foreign-body sensation, stromal inflammation causes haze and decreased vision, endothelial failure causes corneal edema and morning blur that improves through the day, and ectasia causes progressive myopia and irregular astigmatism. The umbrella covers infectious keratitis (bacterial, viral, fungal, Acanthamoeba), inherited dystrophies (Fuchs endothelial, lattice, granular, macular, posterior polymorphous), ectasias (keratoconus, pellucid marginal degeneration, post-LASIK ectasia), mechanical injuries (abrasion, laceration, recurrent corneal erosion), neovascularization, scars and opacities, band keratopathy, limbal stem cell deficiency, and ulcers. Cornea is a recognized subspecialty within ophthalmology, with cornea fellows trained in slit-lamp diagnostics, microbial workup, corneal cross-linking, lamellar and full-thickness transplantation, and ocular surface reconstruction.

typesClassification.description: Inflammation of the cornea caused by bacteria (Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Pseudomonas), viruses (HSV, VZV, adenovirus), fungi (Fusarium, Aspergillus, Candida), or the protozoan Acanthamoeba. Contact-lens wear is the dominant risk in industrialized countries; trauma with vegetable matter dominates in tropical and agricultural settings.

Includes marginal keratitis (immune reaction to staphylococcal antigens in blepharitis), peripheral ulcerative keratitis (associated with rheumatoid arthritis and other systemic autoimmunity), neurotrophic keratitis (loss of corneal sensation), and exposure keratitis (incomplete lid closure).

Bilateral inherited disorders that affect specific layers. Fuchs endothelial dystrophy is the most common, affecting roughly 4% of US adults over 40. Other entities include Cogan microcystic (epithelial basement membrane), lattice, granular, and macular stromal dystrophies, and posterior polymorphous dystrophy.

Progressive thinning and steepening of the cornea. Keratoconus (1 in 375 to 1 in 2,000 in population-based studies) is the most common, followed by pellucid marginal degeneration and post-LASIK ectasia. Onset is typically in the second or third decade.
Failure of endothelial pumping leads to stromal swelling, epithelial bullae, and pain. Most often caused by Fuchs dystrophy or post-surgical pseudophakic bullous keratopathy.

Permanent loss of stromal transparency from healed infection, trauma, or chemical injury. Treatment ranges from rigid gas-permeable contact lenses for irregular astigmatism to lamellar or full-thickness transplantation for dense visual-axis scars.
Mechanical injury to the epithelium (abrasion) or full-thickness wound (laceration). Abrasions heal within 24-72 hours; lacerations require surgical repair and are an ophthalmic emergency.

An infected epithelial defect with underlying stromal infiltrate. A sight-threatening emergency requiring same-day cornea referral, scraping for cultures, and fortified topical antibiotics dosed hourly.

Repeated spontaneous breakdown of the epithelium, classically on first waking, in patients with prior abrasion or epithelial basement membrane dystrophy. Treated stepwise from lubricants to bandage contact lens to anterior stromal puncture or PTK.

Sun-related fibrovascular growth from the conjunctiva onto the cornea. See the dedicated pterygium-of-eye page for diagnosis and excision techniques. Other degenerations include band keratopathy and Salzmann nodular degeneration.

Pathological in-growth of blood vessels into the normally avascular cornea, driven by chronic hypoxia (contact lens overwear), inflammation, or limbal stem cell deficiency. Predisposes to lipid deposition, scarring, and graft rejection.

Loss of the epithelial stem cells at the corneoscleral junction, most often from chemical burns, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, or aniridia. Produces conjunctivalization of the cornea and requires cultivated limbal epithelial transplant or keratoprosthesis.

primarySymptoms: Sharp pain, often described as a feeling of sand or glass in the eye, that worsens with blinking and improves with topical anesthetic in the emergency room.
Foreign-body sensation that persists despite repeated irrigation, classic for epithelial defect, abrasion, or retained subtarsal foreign body.
Photophobia ranging from squinting in normal indoor light to inability to tolerate any illumination, typical of keratitis and uveitis.
Excessive tearing (reflex epiphora) driven by trigeminal stimulation from any corneal surface insult.

Blurred or distorted vision that varies with disease type: morning blur clearing through the day suggests endothelial disease, while progressive myopia and ghosting in young adults suggest keratoconus.
Redness localized to the limbal area (ciliary flush) rather than diffuse conjunctival injection, indicating deeper corneal or intraocular involvement.
Halos and starbursts around lights, especially at night, from corneal edema, irregular astigmatism, or scarring along the visual axis.
A visible white spot, ulcer, or grey infiltrate on the cornea noted in the mirror or by a family member, which always warrants same-day evaluation.
Reduced contact lens tolerance with new pain, redness, or discharge — a red flag for microbial keratitis until proven otherwise.
Recurrent episodes of severe eye pain on first waking, lasting minutes to hours, characteristic of recurrent corneal erosion.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild morning blur that clears within an hour, an early indicator of Fuchs endothelial dystrophy before frank edema develops
Frequent prescription changes in a teenager or young adult, particularly with increasing astigmatism, suggestive of early keratoconus
Persistent foreign-body sensation lasting more than 24 hours after removing a contact lens
New light sensitivity in one eye with watering, especially in a contact-lens wearer who slept in lenses
Recurrent unilateral red eye with shallow ulcers in a dendritic (branching) pattern, suggesting herpes simplex keratitis
emergencySigns: Severe eye pain, photophobia, and visual loss in a contact-lens wearer — possible microbial keratitis requiring same-day cornea evaluation and scrapings
A white infiltrate or ulcer visible on the cornea with hypopyon (layered pus in the front chamber) — vision-threatening infection
Penetrating eye injury or full-thickness corneal laceration with shallow anterior chamber, iris prolapse, or extruded intraocular contents
Chemical splash to the eye, especially with alkali agents (lye, ammonia, cement) — immediate copious irrigation for at least 30 minutes before transport
Sudden severe pain after a tree branch, fingernail, or vegetable-matter injury with progressive infiltrate — high suspicion for fungal or Acanthamoeba keratitis

causes.description: The dominant cause of microbial keratitis in industrialized countries. Overnight wear, poor hygiene, tap water rinsing of cases, and extended-wear schedules increase risk roughly 30-fold compared with no lens wear. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common pathogen in this group.

Abrasions, lacerations, and embedded foreign bodies disrupt the epithelial barrier and allow pathogen entry. Vegetable-matter injuries in agricultural workers carry a particularly high fungal keratitis risk.

Corneal dystrophies follow Mendelian inheritance. TGFBI variants underlie lattice and granular dystrophy; COL8A2 and TCF4 variants are associated with Fuchs endothelial dystrophy. Keratoconus is multifactorial with a substantial heritable component.

Herpes simplex virus persists in the trigeminal ganglion after primary infection and reactivates as dendritic epithelial keratitis or, with deeper involvement, stromal disease. Varicella-zoster reactivation produces herpes zoster ophthalmicus.

Severe dry eye, blepharitis, exposure from incomplete lid closure, and neurotrophic states (post-herpetic, diabetes, fifth nerve injury) erode the corneal surface and predispose to persistent epithelial defects, scarring, and secondary infection.

Rheumatoid arthritis, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, systemic lupus, and relapsing polychondritis can drive peripheral ulcerative keratitis. Sjogren syndrome causes profound aqueous-deficient dry eye with surface breakdown.
Vigorous chronic eye rubbing, particularly in patients with atopic disease, is one of the strongest modifiable risk factors for keratoconus progression.

Lifetime UV exposure contributes to pterygium, climatic droplet keratopathy, and accelerates some corneal degenerations. Equatorial latitudes and outdoor occupations carry the highest cumulative dose.

riskFactors.factor: Contact lens wear, especially overnight or extended-wear
riskFactors.description: Daily-wear soft lens use raises microbial keratitis risk roughly 5-fold; overnight wear raises it 10-20-fold. Rigid gas-permeable lenses are safer; daily disposables are safer than reusable lenses.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of corneal dystrophy or keratoconus
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of keratoconus patients have a 15-67-fold higher risk depending on the population studied. Fuchs dystrophy follows autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance.
Endothelial cell density declines from approximately 3,500 cells/mm2 at birth to 2,000-2,500 in middle age; Fuchs dystrophy and pseudophakic bullous keratopathy both present after age 50.

riskFactors.factor: Atopic disease and chronic eye rubbing
riskFactors.description: Atopic dermatitis, allergic conjunctivitis, and vernal keratoconjunctivitis predispose to keratoconus through repeated mechanical rubbing. Treating allergy and behavioural counselling on no-rub reduce progression.
Vegetable-matter trauma is the leading cause of fungal keratitis in tropical and rural regions. UV exposure adds risk of pterygium and climatic droplet keratopathy.

riskFactors.factor: History of herpes simplex or zoster ophthalmicus
riskFactors.description: Once trigeminal latency is established, recurrence rates approach 50% within 10 years of the first episode. Suppressive oral acyclovir reduces recurrence by about 50% (HEDS trial).
Diabetic neuropathy reduces corneal sensation, slows epithelial healing, and increases risk of neurotrophic keratitis and persistent epithelial defects after surgery.
Removal of stromal tissue can unmask sub-clinical ectasia. Modern preoperative tomography screening has substantially reduced but not eliminated this complication.

riskFactors.factor: Previous chemical or thermal eye injury
riskFactors.description: Alkali burns destroy limbal stem cells and produce chronic surface failure. Even years later, patients remain at risk of recurrent erosions, scarring, and neovascularization.
HIV, organ transplant, and chronic steroid use increase the severity of viral and fungal keratitis and slow healing of any corneal insult.

affectedDemographics: Global blindness data (WHO 2019): corneal opacity is the fourth leading cause of blindness worldwide, affecting roughly 4.2 million people
US data: 80,000-85,000 corneal transplants per year (Eye Bank Association of America 2023); endothelial keratoplasty now accounts for over 60% of transplants
Keratoconus prevalence: 1 in 2,000 in older clinical estimates; recent population-based studies using corneal tomography report 1 in 375 in Dutch and Saudi cohorts
Fuchs dystrophy is roughly 3 times more common in women than men and is strongly age-dependent
Microbial keratitis disproportionately affects working-age adults in low-income tropical countries, where access to topical antifungals and surgical care is limited
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of corneal disease begins at the slit-lamp, the cornea specialist's primary instrument. A careful history (contact lens use, trauma, previous herpes episodes, family history, occupation) plus visual acuity, intraocular pressure, and slit-lamp examination establishes the diagnosis in most cases. Fluorescein staining highlights epithelial defects and dendritic herpes lesions; rose bengal or lissamine green stains devitalized epithelial cells in dry eye and exposure. Pattern recognition is central: a single white infiltrate with overlying epithelial defect and anterior chamber reaction signals microbial keratitis and triggers corneal scraping for Gram stain, KOH or calcofluor, and culture on blood, chocolate, and Sabouraud agar — the diagnostic gold standard for infectious keratitis. PCR for herpes simplex confirms atypical dendrites. Corneal topography and Scheimpflug tomography (Pentacam, Galilei) quantify anterior and posterior surface curvature and are essential for diagnosing keratoconus, screening pre-refractive-surgery candidates, and monitoring ectasia progression. Anterior segment OCT provides cross-sectional imaging of epithelium, stroma, and Descemet's membrane and is used to plan lamellar surgery, assess graft attachment, and characterise dystrophies. Specular and confocal microscopy measure endothelial cell density (normal above 2,000 cells per square millimeter; Fuchs patients often below 1,000 by the time of symptoms) and can visualise Acanthamoeba cysts in vivo. For autoimmune-associated peripheral ulcerative keratitis, systemic workup including ANA, ANCA, rheumatoid factor, and CRP is mandatory because untreated systemic vasculitis carries high mortality. Septic perforation, scleritis, and endophthalmitis are the critical differentials that must be excluded in any acutely painful red eye with visual loss.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The foundational corneal examination. Identifies epithelial defects, ulcers, dendrites, infiltrates, edema, neovascularization, scars, and foreign bodies. Anterior chamber reaction (cells, flare, hypopyon) is graded simultaneously.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient sits at a microscope, places chin and forehead on rests, and looks at fixation targets while the clinician shines a thin beam across the cornea. A drop of fluorescein dye and cobalt blue light highlights surface defects. Painless, takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Corneal scraping for Gram stain, KOH, and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for microbial keratitis. Identifies bacterial, fungal, and Acanthamoeba pathogens to guide targeted therapy and predicts antibiotic susceptibility.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After topical anesthetic, a sterile spatula or 21-gauge needle is used to gently scrape the leading edge and base of the ulcer. Samples are plated directly on culture media at the slit-lamp. Brief stinging is normal; results return in 24-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps anterior and posterior corneal curvature and thickness across the entire surface. Essential for diagnosing keratoconus, monitoring cross-linking response, screening refractive surgery candidates, and detecting post-LASIK ectasia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient fixates on a target while a rotating camera or Placido disc captures the cornea. Completely painless and contact-free; takes 1-2 minutes per eye.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High-resolution cross-sectional imaging of the cornea down to micrometer scale. Used to measure epithelial thickness, characterise dystrophies, plan DSAEK/DMEK depth, and confirm graft attachment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact scan at the slit-lamp or a dedicated AS-OCT machine. Painless; takes a few minutes per eye.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Specular microscopy measures endothelial cell density and morphology — critical for Fuchs and pre-cataract assessment. Confocal microscopy visualises Acanthamoeba cysts, fungal hyphae, and dystrophic deposits in vivo.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact specular microscopy is brief and painless. Confocal microscopy involves a probe touching the anesthetised cornea for about 1-2 minutes per eye.

diagnosticTests.test: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for herpes simplex virus
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms HSV in atypical dendrites, neurotrophic ulcers, and persistent stromal keratitis where clinical pattern alone is ambiguous.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab or epithelial scraping is sent for PCR; results return in 1-3 days depending on the lab.

diagnosticTests.test: Systemic autoimmune workup (ANA, ANCA, rheumatoid factor, CRP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Required in peripheral ulcerative keratitis, which can be the presenting feature of granulomatosis with polyangiitis or untreated rheumatoid arthritis with high mortality if not addressed systemically.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests drawn at the same visit. Results return in 1-7 days depending on the panel.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of corneal disease is layered: protect the surface, treat the underlying disease, restore structure, and replace tissue only when necessary. Lubrication with preservative-free artificial tears is the foundation across nearly every corneal diagnosis and reduces friction-driven epithelial breakdown. Microbial keratitis is a sight-threatening emergency: empiric fortified vancomycin and tobramycin (or a high-concentration fluoroquinolone) are started hourly around the clock immediately after scraping, then narrowed once cultures return. Fungal keratitis is treated with topical natamycin 5% or voriconazole; Acanthamoeba requires polyhexamethylene biguanide and chlorhexidine for months. Herpes simplex epithelial keratitis responds to oral acyclovir 400 mg five times daily or topical ganciclovir gel; stromal disease additionally requires topical steroid under cover. Neurotrophic keratitis uses autologous serum tears, recombinant human nerve growth factor (cenegermin), and amniotic membrane (PROKERA self-retaining cryopreserved or Tutoplast dehydrated). Corneal cross-linking with riboflavin and ultraviolet-A (Wollensak 2003; FDA-approved Avedro accelerated protocol 2016) halts keratoconus progression in approximately 95% of treated eyes and is offered to anyone with documented progression on tomography. Optical rehabilitation in keratoconus uses rigid gas-permeable lenses, hybrid lenses, or scleral lenses. When the cornea is too distorted or opaque to function optically, transplantation is offered: DSAEK or DMEK for endothelial disease (the latter restores vision faster and rejects less often, EBAA 2023 data), DALK for stromal disease that spares the endothelium, and full-thickness penetrating keratoplasty (PK) for combined or full-thickness involvement. Limbal stem cell deficiency is treated with cultivated limbal epithelial transplant (CLET) or, in end-stage cases, a Boston keratoprosthesis. Pterygium, recurrent erosion, and band keratopathy each have dedicated surgical pathways linked from this page. Strict trigger avoidance — no eye rubbing in keratoconus, no overnight contact lens wear, UV protection outdoors — is part of every treatment plan.

medicalTreatments.name: Preservative-free artificial tears and ocular surface lubricants
medicalTreatments.description: Foundational therapy across dry eye, neurotrophic disease, recurrent erosion, and post-operative care. Preservatives such as benzalkonium chloride worsen epithelial toxicity in chronic use; single-dose unit vials are preferred.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief in 70-80% of mild-to-moderate ocular surface disease; foundational adjunct in all severe corneal disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Fortified topical antibiotics (vancomycin 25 mg/mL plus tobramycin 14 mg/mL, or moxifloxacin 0.5%)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line empiric therapy for bacterial keratitis. Dosed every 30-60 minutes for the first 48 hours then tapered based on culture results and clinical response. Hospital admission is considered for central ulcers, large infiltrates, or non-adherent patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sterilizes infection and arrests stromal melt in 80-90% of cases when started promptly; central ulcers heal with some residual scar.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antiviral therapy (ganciclovir 0.15% gel or oral acyclovir 400 mg five times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard treatment for HSV epithelial keratitis. Oral acyclovir 400 mg twice daily is used for suppression in patients with recurrent disease (HEDS trial showed roughly 50% reduction in recurrence).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves epithelial dendrites in 7-10 days in 90% of patients; long-term suppression halves recurrence rate.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antifungals (natamycin 5%, voriconazole 1%)
medicalTreatments.description: Natamycin is the only FDA-approved topical antifungal in the US and is first-line for filamentous fungi (Fusarium, Aspergillus). Voriconazole is added for resistant cases or yeast infections. Treatment is typically 6-12 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate of 60-80% with timely treatment; perforation and graft requirement remain higher than in bacterial keratitis.

medicalTreatments.name: Acanthamoeba protocol (polyhexamethylene biguanide 0.02% plus chlorhexidine 0.02%)
medicalTreatments.description: Dual-agent biguanide therapy is the standard of care; treatment is dosed hourly initially and continued for 3-6 months. Pain is severe and often disproportionate to clinical signs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eradicates infection in roughly 70% of cases when diagnosed early; delayed diagnosis is associated with poor visual outcomes and need for transplantation.

medicalTreatments.name: Autologous serum tears (20% serum in saline)
medicalTreatments.description: Patient's own blood serum, processed and diluted, contains epidermal and nerve growth factors absent in pharmaceutical lubricants. Used in neurotrophic keratitis, severe dry eye, persistent epithelial defects, and Sjogren syndrome.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Promotes healing in 60-80% of persistent epithelial defects refractory to standard lubricants.

medicalTreatments.name: Cenegermin (recombinant human nerve growth factor, 0.002%)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved (2018) for moderate to severe neurotrophic keratitis. Dosed six times daily for 8 weeks. Restores corneal innervation and supports epithelial healing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete corneal healing in roughly 70% of patients at 8 weeks in the REPARO trials, versus 28% on vehicle.

medicalTreatments.name: Corneal cross-linking (riboflavin 0.1% plus UV-A 365 nm)
medicalTreatments.description: Introduced by Wollensak 2003; FDA-approved Avedro epi-off protocol 2016. Riboflavin is instilled to saturate the stroma, then UV-A is applied for 30 minutes (standard Dresden protocol) or 10 minutes at higher fluence (accelerated). Photochemical crosslinks stiffen collagen and arrest ectasia progression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Halts keratoconus progression in approximately 95% of treated eyes at 5 years; modest flattening of the cone occurs in some patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Amniotic membrane (PROKERA self-retaining cryopreserved or sutured Tutoplast)
medicalTreatments.description: Acts as a biological bandage and anti-inflammatory scaffold. Used for persistent epithelial defects, chemical burns, Stevens-Johnson, neurotrophic ulcers, and post-PK epithelial healing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Accelerates epithelial closure in 70-90% of persistent epithelial defects.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1%, loteprednol 0.5%)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for stromal HSV keratitis, marginal keratitis, postoperative graft, and after the active microbial phase of bacterial ulcers. Contraindicated as monotherapy in active epithelial herpes and untreated microbial keratitis because they impair clearance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Substantially reduces stromal scarring and graft rejection rates when used appropriately under cornea specialist supervision.

surgicalOptions.name: Penetrating keratoplasty (PK)
surgicalOptions.description: Full-thickness corneal transplant. Replaces all five corneal layers; reserved for combined or full-thickness disease such as advanced keratoconus with scarring, failed graft, or post-infectious scar. Visual recovery is 6-12 months with sutures gradually removed over 1-2 years.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft survival at 5 years is approximately 80-90% in keratoconus and around 60% in higher-risk indications (Eye Bank Association of America 2023).

surgicalOptions.name: Descemet stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK)
surgicalOptions.description: Replaces only the diseased endothelium and posterior stroma. Standard treatment for Fuchs dystrophy and pseudophakic bullous keratopathy. Visual rehabilitation in 3-6 months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft survival at 5 years is approximately 90% in Fuchs dystrophy; rejection rate around 10%.

surgicalOptions.name: Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK)
surgicalOptions.description: Replaces only Descemet's membrane and endothelium — the thinnest possible lamellar graft. Faster and more complete visual recovery than DSAEK with lower rejection rates.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 75% of patients achieve 20/25 or better at 6 months; rejection rate around 1-2% per year, the lowest of any keratoplasty.

surgicalOptions.name: Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK)
surgicalOptions.description: Replaces anterior cornea while preserving the patient's own healthy endothelium. Indicated for keratoconus and stromal scars when the endothelium is intact. Eliminates the risk of endothelial rejection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual outcomes comparable to PK in keratoconus, with substantially lower long-term graft failure rates.

surgicalOptions.name: Corneal cross-linking (CXL)
surgicalOptions.description: Outpatient procedure performed under topical anesthetic. Epi-off (standard Dresden) or epi-on protocols; treatment time 30-90 minutes total. Used for progressive keratoconus, pellucid marginal degeneration, and post-LASIK ectasia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Halts ectasia progression in roughly 95% of treated eyes at 5 years (Wollensak 2003 and subsequent trials).

surgicalOptions.name: Phototherapeutic keratectomy (PTK)
surgicalOptions.description: Excimer laser ablation of superficial corneal pathology — used for recurrent erosions, anterior dystrophies, and superficial scars. Often combined with mitomycin C to reduce haze.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Eliminates recurrent erosion in 80-90% of patients with epithelial basement membrane dystrophy.

surgicalOptions.name: Limbal stem cell transplant (CLET, KLAL, SLET)
surgicalOptions.description: Restores corneal epithelium in limbal stem cell deficiency from chemical burns, Stevens-Johnson, or aniridia. Cultivated limbal epithelial transplant (CLET) and simple limbal epithelial transplant (SLET) are the modern techniques.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surface stability in 70-80% of unilateral chemical burn cases at 3 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Boston keratoprosthesis (Kpro)
surgicalOptions.description: Artificial cornea reserved for patients with multiple failed grafts, severe limbal stem cell deficiency, or autoimmune surface disease where biological grafts cannot survive.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Retention of the device at 5 years is roughly 70%; functional vision is achievable in patients otherwise considered un-transplantable.

surgicalOptions.name: Pterygium excision with conjunctival autograft
surgicalOptions.description: See the dedicated pterygium-of-eye page for indications, technique, and recurrence rates. Conjunctival autograft is now standard and has reduced recurrence to under 10%.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence below 10% with autograft, versus 30-50% with bare sclera technique.

alternativeTreatments.name: Scleral contact lenses
alternativeTreatments.description: Large-diameter rigid lenses that vault the cornea and rest on the sclera, creating a fluid reservoir. Highly effective for keratoconus, post-PK irregular astigmatism, severe dry eye, and exposure keratopathy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intrastromal corneal ring segments (ICRS, Intacs)
alternativeTreatments.description: Two thin polymethyl methacrylate arcs inserted into the mid-peripheral stroma to flatten and re-center a keratoconic cornea. Adjunct to cross-linking in selected patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Lifestyle measures: no eye rubbing, UV protection, contact lens hygiene
alternativeTreatments.description: Eliminating eye rubbing slows keratoconus progression. Daily disposable lenses and elimination of overnight wear reduce microbial keratitis risk. Wraparound UV-blocking sunglasses reduce pterygium recurrence and climatic droplet keratopathy.

whySeeSpecialist: A cornea specialist (fellowship-trained ophthalmologist) should be involved whenever there is a suspected infectious ulcer, progressive ectasia on tomography, declining vision from endothelial disease, persistent epithelial defect beyond 2 weeks, recurrent erosions failing medical therapy, suspected dystrophy on slit-lamp, or any consideration of corneal transplantation. General ophthalmology can manage uncomplicated abrasions, allergic eye disease, and simple dry eye, but should refer early for anything that does not heal as expected or threatens central vision.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for fellowship training in cornea and external disease, hospital privileges that include corneal transplantation, and access to corneal tomography (Pentacam or equivalent), AS-OCT, specular microscopy, and corneal cross-linking. For complex cases, choose centers that perform DMEK regularly — volume correlates with outcomes. Ask about cross-linking protocol options (epi-on vs epi-off, standard vs accelerated), pediatric experience for early-onset keratoconus, and whether the center participates in eye bank programs.
preventionStrategies: Follow strict contact lens hygiene: daily disposables where feasible, no overnight wear, no tap water rinsing of cases, replace cases every 3 months
Wear ANSI Z87.1-rated safety glasses or goggles for power tools, grass-cutting, sports, and chemical handling
Treat blepharitis and dry eye proactively with lid hygiene, warm compresses, and lubricants to maintain a healthy ocular surface
Avoid eye rubbing, especially in atopic patients and anyone with diagnosed or suspected keratoconus
Use wraparound UV-blocking sunglasses outdoors, particularly in equatorial and high-altitude regions, to reduce pterygium and surface degeneration
Seek same-day evaluation for any contact-lens wearer with new red eye, pain, or blurred vision rather than continuing lens wear
lifestyleModifications: Switch from extended-wear or overnight contact lenses to daily disposables, and never sleep in lenses unless specifically prescribed
Use a humidifier in heated or air-conditioned environments to reduce evaporative dry eye and surface stress
Adjust screen workstation ergonomics: 20-20-20 rule (every 20 minutes look 20 feet away for 20 seconds), monitor below eye level, conscious blinking
Address sleep apnea if present, since CPAP mask leak and floppy eyelid syndrome both contribute to chronic ocular surface disease
Maintain glycemic control in diabetes to preserve corneal nerve function and epithelial healing capacity
In keratoconus, eliminate eye rubbing actively, treat atopy aggressively, and review contact lens fit regularly

recommended: Oily fish twice weekly (salmon, mackerel, sardines) for omega-3 fatty acids, modestly beneficial in dry eye disease
Leafy greens and orange or yellow vegetables for lutein, zeaxanthin, and vitamin A precursors essential for ocular surface health
Adequate water intake (2 liters daily unless restricted) to support tear production
Vitamin A-rich foods (liver, eggs, dairy, sweet potato) to prevent vitamin A deficiency keratomalacia, still a major cause of pediatric corneal blindness globally
avoid: Severe calorie restriction or fad diets in regions with limited animal-source food, which can precipitate vitamin A deficiency
Excessive caffeine and alcohol, which both worsen evaporative dry eye
Smoking, which accelerates dry eye, slows corneal nerve regeneration, and worsens nearly every ocular surface disease

exerciseGuidelines: Routine aerobic and resistance exercise is safe and recommended in all corneal disease. After corneal transplant, avoid water-line activities (swimming, hot tubs) and heavy lifting that triggers Valsalva for 4-6 weeks. Eye protection is essential for ball sports and racquet sports throughout life if any corneal pathology is present, and is mandatory for the rest of life after a transplant or laceration repair.
dailyManagement: Use prescribed lubricants and medications on a consistent schedule — adherence is the strongest predictor of stable disease in chronic surface conditions
Track vision and symptoms; any sudden change warrants same-week ophthalmology contact
Replace contact lenses and cases on schedule, and never top up old solution
Wear protective eyewear for any activity with projectile risk, particularly after corneal surgery
Keep a list of current eye drops, allergies, and date of last cornea specialist visit accessible during travel
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the specific corneal disease and timing of treatment. Simple abrasions heal within 24-72 hours with no long-term consequence. Promptly treated bacterial keratitis usually resolves with limited scarring and recovery of vision within weeks; delayed presentation or central ulcers may leave permanent scar requiring transplantation. Keratoconus stabilises with cross-linking in roughly 95% of treated eyes and most patients retain functional vision lifelong with appropriate contact lenses. Fuchs dystrophy progresses slowly over decades; modern DMEK restores 20/25 vision or better in approximately 75% of patients at 6 months with 5-year graft survival around 90%. Herpes simplex keratitis is recurrent in nature, but suppressive oral acyclovir halves the recurrence rate and most patients retain useful vision lifelong. The worst prognosis is reserved for advanced limbal stem cell deficiency, Stevens-Johnson surface failure, and end-stage neurotrophic keratitis, where multiple surgical interventions may be needed and final vision is limited. Across the umbrella, the single greatest determinant of outcome is time to cornea specialist — every day of delayed treatment in microbial keratitis or fungal infection meaningfully increases the likelihood of perforation, transplant requirement, and permanent vision loss.
recoveryTimeline: Abrasions epithelialise within 24-72 hours. Bacterial keratitis sterilises within 5-7 days of starting fortified antibiotics; residual stromal haze remodels over months. Cross-linking re-epithelialises in 4-7 days with vision typically blurry for 4-6 weeks before stabilising. DMEK vision improves over 3-6 months, DSAEK over 6 months, and PK over 6-12 months with suture removal continuing to 18-24 months. Fungal and Acanthamoeba keratitis can require 3-6 months of treatment with prolonged surveillance afterwards.
complications: Corneal perforation requiring emergency tissue adhesive, patch graft, or penetrating keratoplasty — most common with delayed-treated bacterial, fungal, or rheumatoid ulcers
Permanent corneal scar in the visual axis with irreversible vision loss — preventable in most cases with prompt antimicrobial treatment
Endophthalmitis (intraocular infection) following untreated full-thickness ulcer or perforation — a sight-threatening emergency
Glaucoma secondary to inflammation, steroid response, or graft surgery — present in 10-20% of long-term post-PK patients
Graft rejection or failure after transplantation, requiring repeat surgery in 10-30% depending on indication and graft type
Recurrent corneal erosion, neurotrophic ulcer, or persistent epithelial defect with chronic pain and disability
Posterior segment complications including macular edema and retinal detachment after extensive anterior segment surgery

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Cornea and External Disease
supportResources.description: Patient-facing summaries of every major corneal condition from the same body that writes the Preferred Practice Patterns clinicians follow.

supportResources.name: National Keratoconus Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient community, contact lens fitting resources, and updates on cross-linking and surgical advances.

supportResources.name: Eye Bank Association of America
supportResources.description: US-focused information on corneal transplantation, donor process, and graft outcomes statistics.

supportResources.name: Cornea Society
supportResources.description: Professional society for cornea specialists; patient education content and find-a-specialist directory.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pterygium
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pterygium is a sun-induced fibrovascular growth from the conjunctiva onto the peripheral cornea. It has a distinct triangular shape, grows slowly over years, and rarely affects vision until it crosses the visual axis. Most corneal disorders involve the cornea itself rather than a conjunctival outgrowth, and present with pain or sudden visual change.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma is a progressive optic neuropathy driven by intraocular pressure, with vision loss starting peripherally and a normal-appearing cornea on slit-lamp until very advanced. Corneal disorders affect the cornea itself and usually cause pain, redness, photophobia, or central visual blur — symptoms that are absent in early glaucoma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataract
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataract is opacification of the crystalline lens behind the iris, producing gradual painless visual decline and glare. The cornea is clear on examination. Corneal disorders affect the front of the eye and typically produce pain, redness, or surface findings that cataract does not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Age-related Macular Degeneration
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Macular degeneration is a retinal disease producing central visual distortion and scotomas with a normal cornea. Corneal disorders are diagnosed at the slit-lamp on the front of the eye, while macular degeneration is diagnosed on dilated fundus exam and OCT of the retina.

relatedConditions.name: Pterygium
relatedConditions.relevance: A common corneal-conjunctival condition with its own dedicated page; pterygium excision and cornea care overlap closely.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists with corneal disease; long-term topical steroid use after keratitis or transplant can elevate intraocular pressure.

relatedConditions.name: Cataract
relatedConditions.relevance: Cataract surgery and corneal endothelial health are tightly linked — Fuchs patients often need staged cataract and DMEK procedures.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Profound aqueous-deficient dry eye in Sjogren patients drives corneal surface breakdown, filamentary keratitis, and persistent epithelial defects.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetic neuropathy reduces corneal sensation and slows epithelial healing; diabetic keratopathy can complicate any anterior segment surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes Simplex Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Herpetic eye disease is one of the most common viral corneal conditions and requires lifelong vigilance for recurrence.

faqs.question: What is the cornea and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: The cornea is the clear dome at the front of the eye that lets light enter and provides about two-thirds of the eye's focusing power. It is avascular, richly innervated by the trigeminal nerve, and only about 540 micrometers thick centrally. Damage to its shape, surface, or transparency immediately reduces vision and often causes severe pain.

faqs.question: What are the main types of corneal disease?
faqs.answer: Corneal disorders include infectious keratitis (bacterial, viral, fungal, Acanthamoeba), dystrophies (Fuchs, lattice, granular), ectasias (keratoconus, pellucid marginal degeneration, post-LASIK ectasia), edema and decompensation, scars and opacities, abrasions and lacerations, ulcers, recurrent erosions, pterygium, neovascularization, and limbal stem cell deficiency.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a corneal problem?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms are pain, foreign-body sensation, light sensitivity, tearing, redness, blurred vision, and halos around lights. Sudden severe pain with vision loss in a contact-lens wearer is a red flag for microbial keratitis. Morning blur that clears through the day suggests Fuchs endothelial dystrophy.

faqs.question: Is keratitis the same as a corneal ulcer?
faqs.answer: Keratitis is any inflammation of the cornea. A corneal ulcer specifically describes an epithelial defect with an underlying stromal infiltrate, almost always caused by infection. All corneal ulcers are a form of keratitis, but not all keratitis is ulcerative — for example, sterile marginal keratitis or viral epithelial keratitis.

faqs.question: How is microbial keratitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made at the slit-lamp by identifying an epithelial defect with a stromal infiltrate. Corneal scraping for Gram stain, KOH, and culture on blood, chocolate, and Sabouraud agar is the gold standard. PCR is used for herpes simplex when clinical pattern alone is ambiguous.

faqs.question: Can contact lenses cause corneal damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Contact lens wear is the leading cause of microbial keratitis in industrialized countries, raising risk roughly 5-fold for daily wear and 10-20-fold for overnight wear. Safer practices include daily disposables, never sleeping in lenses, never rinsing cases with tap water, and replacing cases every 3 months.

faqs.question: What is keratoconus?
faqs.answer: Keratoconus is progressive thinning and steepening of the cornea, producing irregular astigmatism, progressive myopia, and ghost images. It typically begins between ages 10 and 25, affects roughly 1 in 375 to 1 in 2,000 people, and is strongly associated with chronic eye rubbing and atopic disease.

faqs.question: Does corneal cross-linking work for keratoconus?
faqs.answer: Yes. Corneal cross-linking with riboflavin and ultraviolet-A halts keratoconus progression in roughly 95% of treated eyes at 5 years. The original Dresden protocol was published by Wollensak in 2003; the FDA approved an accelerated protocol in 2016. Cross-linking does not improve vision but prevents further loss.

faqs.question: What is Fuchs endothelial dystrophy?
faqs.answer: Fuchs endothelial dystrophy is an inherited disease where the endothelial cells that pump water out of the cornea fail progressively. It affects roughly 4% of US adults over 40 and is three times more common in women. Symptoms start as morning blur that clears through the day; advanced disease causes constant haze and bullous pain.

faqs.question: What is the difference between DSAEK and DMEK?
faqs.answer: DSAEK transplants Descemet's membrane, the endothelium, and a thin layer of posterior stroma. DMEK transplants only Descemet's membrane and endothelium. DMEK gives faster and more complete visual recovery with rejection rates around 1-2% per year, but is technically more demanding to prepare and implant.

faqs.question: How long does a corneal transplant last?
faqs.answer: Graft survival depends on indication and graft type. In keratoconus, full-thickness penetrating keratoplasty grafts have 5-year survival around 80-90%. DMEK for Fuchs dystrophy reaches around 90% at 5 years with the lowest rejection rates of any keratoplasty. High-risk cases such as repeat grafts or chemical burns have lower survival.

faqs.question: What should I do for a corneal abrasion at home?
faqs.answer: After a minor abrasion, avoid rubbing, do not patch the eye, and use preservative-free lubricant drops frequently. Avoid contact lens wear until healed. See an eye care professional within 24 hours for evaluation, topical antibiotic prescription, and review for retained foreign body. Most abrasions heal within 24-72 hours.

faqs.question: Is herpes eye disease curable?
faqs.answer: Herpes simplex keratitis is not curable because the virus persists in the trigeminal ganglion, but it is highly controllable. Acute episodes are treated with oral or topical antivirals and resolve within 7-10 days. Suppressive oral acyclovir 400 mg twice daily reduces recurrence by approximately 50% based on the HEDS trial.

faqs.question: When is a corneal disorder a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Severe eye pain with vision loss, especially in a contact-lens wearer; a visible white spot or ulcer on the cornea; layered pus in the front chamber (hypopyon); penetrating eye injury; or any chemical splash to the eye. Chemical injuries require immediate copious irrigation for at least 30 minutes before transport to an emergency department.

faqs.question: Can corneal disease cause blindness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Corneal opacity is the fourth leading cause of global blindness, after cataract, glaucoma, and age-related macular degeneration. Untreated microbial keratitis can perforate the eye within days. Most corneal-related blindness is preventable or reversible with timely access to a cornea specialist.

faqs.question: Does dry eye damage the cornea?
faqs.answer: Severe and chronic dry eye, particularly the aqueous-deficient type in Sjogren syndrome, damages the corneal epithelium and can produce filamentary keratitis, persistent epithelial defects, and rarely sterile melt. Most mild and moderate dry eye does not cause permanent corneal damage when treated with lubricants and lid hygiene.

faqs.question: How is keratoconus screened before LASIK?
faqs.answer: Pre-LASIK screening uses corneal tomography with the Pentacam or similar instrument, which maps anterior and posterior surface curvature and pachymetry. Patients with thin corneas, asymmetric astigmatism, or abnormal posterior elevation are excluded or offered surface ablation or implantable collamer lenses instead. Screening has substantially reduced but not eliminated post-LASIK ectasia.

faqs.question: What is recurrent corneal erosion?
faqs.answer: Recurrent corneal erosion is repeated breakdown of the epithelium, usually on first waking, in patients with a prior abrasion or epithelial basement membrane dystrophy. Treatment is stepwise: nighttime lubricant ointment, hypertonic saline drops, bandage contact lens, and for refractory cases anterior stromal puncture or phototherapeutic keratectomy.

faqs.question: Can children have corneal disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children can develop infectious keratitis from trauma or contact lens use, congenital dystrophies (CHED, posterior polymorphous), ocular surface failure from Stevens-Johnson syndrome, and vitamin A deficiency keratomalacia in low-income regions. Pediatric keratoconus tends to progress faster than adult disease and warrants early cross-linking.

faqs.question: What costs are associated with corneal treatment?
faqs.answer: Lubricants and topical antibiotics are inexpensive generics. Topical antifungals and Acanthamoeba agents are costly and often imported. Corneal cross-linking ranges widely by country. Corneal transplantation costs are dominated by surgical and donor tissue fees, with DMEK and DSAEK typically less expensive in total than penetrating keratoplasty due to shorter recovery.

faqs.question: Will I need glasses or contact lenses after a corneal transplant?
faqs.answer: Most patients need glasses, rigid gas-permeable lenses, or scleral lenses after a corneal transplant because some residual astigmatism is almost universal. After DMEK, around 75% of patients see 20/25 or better with glasses alone. After penetrating keratoplasty, rigid or scleral lenses are often needed for best vision.

simpleName: Corneal disorders (diseases of the front window of the eye)
metaDescription: Corneal disorders: causes, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatments including cross-linking, DMEK, and corneal transplant, reviewed by our medical board.
